interactions.summary: Jiang says the North wins because free labor makes it more populous, wealthy, and energetic, then argues that capitalism's money concentration after frontier closure forces America to scale its game globally through Bretton Woods, the dollar reserve system, and a gold peg.

He maps the institutions that spread and coordinate the American world game: World Bank loans, IMF privatization discipline, GATT/WTO free trade, SWIFT bank transfer infrastructure, and the secretive Bank of International Settlements.
He says U.S. overextension through Vietnam, the space race, and the Great Society breaks the gold promise in 1971, turning the dollar into a confidence-dependent Ponzi scheme.

speaker_notes.note: Continuous Jiang exposition; repeated 'does that make sense' checks are rhetorical and receive no separate student response.

claims.claim: Jiang says capitalism's internal problem is that if the game is played long enough, one person or a few people control all the money.
He claims the Great Depression and frontier closure force America to conquer and scale the game to the world after World War II.
Bretton Woods establishes the world game by making the U.S. dollar the reserve currency, with other currencies deriving value through their relationship to it.
The dollar's gold peg is presented as a stabilizer that prevents the Americans from printing unlimited money inside the reserve-currency system.
Jiang says the World Bank and IMF spread the game by first lending to poor countries and then forcing policy conformity, especially privatization, when debt becomes unpayable.
He frames GATT/WTO, SWIFT, and the Bank of International Settlements as coordination infrastructure for free trade, dollar-denominated bank transfers, and central-bank policy alignment.
Nixon ending dollar-gold redemption in 1971 turns the dollar, in Jiang's words, into a Ponzi scheme whose value depends on people wanting to use it.

signature_moments.moment: Capitalism's game ends with one player holding all the money unless the board expands.
signature_moments.source_phrase: at the end of the game, one person controls all the money
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This explains why American expansion is not incidental but required by the game's own logic.

signature_moments.moment: The World Bank gets countries into the game; the IMF changes them when they cannot pay.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the IMF comes in and changes your government
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the institutional machinery of the world game a blunt two-step mechanism.

signature_moments.moment: After 1971, the dollar is valuable because everyone still wants to use it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the U.S. dollar is valuable because people want to use it
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's sharpest description of post-gold dollar power as pure demand management.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The conference Jiang presents as establishing the American world game through the U.S. dollar reserve-currency system.
An institution Jiang says spreads the game by giving loans that let poor countries participate.
An institution Jiang says changes indebted countries' governments and policies to conform to the game, especially through privatization.
Banking coordination infrastructure Jiang describes as enabling international money transfers through the dollar-centered system.
Jiang's label for the private central-bank coordination body where countries align economic policy.
Jiang's provocative term for the post-1971 dollar: valuable because people want to use it rather than because it can be redeemed for gold.

chronology_notes.note: This packet builds a dated historical sequence from Civil War and frontier closure to 1944 Bretton Woods, 1945 U.S. victory, institutional expansion, and the 1971 Nixon shock.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders renminbi as 'room B' and may garble 'Bank for International Settlements' as 'Bank of International Settlements'; the institutional references are clear from context.
